84. ## Code Quality Standards
85. 
86. ### Formatting & Style
87. 
88. - Tab indentation (configured in formatter)
89. - Double quotes for strings
90. - Semicolons required
91. - `npm run format` MUST pass before commit
92. - `npm run check` MUST pass before merge
93. 
94. ### Biome Linting Rules (MANDATORY)
95. 
96. These rules are enforced by our linter and MUST be followed:
97. 
98. #### Performance Rules
99. 
100. - **useTopLevelRegex** (CRITICAL): Regular expressions MUST be defined at the top level (as constants) outside functions
101.   - ❌ BAD: `const name = filename.replace(/\.agent\.md$/, "")`
102.   - ❌ BAD: `expect(path).toMatch(/\.agent\.md$/)`
103.   - ✅ GOOD: Define `const AGENT_FILE_PATTERN = /\.agent\.md$/;` at top level, then use `filename.replace(AGENT_FILE_PATTERN, "")`
104.   - **Reason**: Regex literals inside functions are recompiled on every call, causing performance degradation
105.   - **Rule applies to**: Production code, test files, and ANY file where regex is used inside a function/loop
106.   - **How to fix**:
107.     1. Move regex to top-level constant with descriptive name
108.     2. Use SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE for the constant name
109.     3. Add a comment if the pattern isn't obvious
110.   - **Examples of affected locations**:
111.     - Inside `describe()` or `it()` test blocks
112.     - Inside class methods
113.     - Inside arrow functions or callbacks
114.     - Inside loops
115. 
116. #### Code Quality Rules
117. 
118. - **noShadow**: Variables MUST NOT shadow outer scope variables with the same name
119.   - ❌ BAD:
120. 
121.     ```typescript
122.     const agents = getAgents();
123.     callback((agents) => { /* shadows outer 'agents' */ });
124.     ```
125. 
126.   - ✅ GOOD:
127. 
128.     ```typescript
129.     const agents = getAgents();
130.     callback((agentList) => { /* different name */ });
131.     ```
132. 
133.   - Reason: Variable shadowing makes code confusing and error-prone
134. 
135. - **noExcessiveCognitiveComplexity** (CRITICAL): Functions MUST NOT exceed complexity score of 15
136.   - **What is complexity**: Nested conditions, loops, try-catch, logical operators, early returns
137.   - **How to measure**: Biome calculates complexity automatically
138.   - **How to fix**:
139.     1. Extract helper functions for logical blocks
140.     2. Use early returns to reduce nesting
141.     3. Extract complex conditions into well-named boolean variables
142.     4. Split validation/processing into separate functions
143.   - **Example pattern for refactoring**:
144. 
145.     ```typescript
146.     // ❌ BAD: High complexity (21)
147.     async validateData(data: Data): Promise<Result> {
148.       const errors = [];
149.       if (!data || typeof data !== "object") {
150.         errors.push({field: "data", message: "Invalid"});
151.         return {valid: false, errors};
152.       }
153.       if (!data.name || data.name.length === 0) {
154.         errors.push({field: "name", message: "Empty"});
155.       } else if (data.name.length > 100) {
156.         errors.push({field: "name", message: "Too long"});
157.       }
158.       if (data.type === "custom") {
159.         if (data.params?.subtype) {
160.           const validTypes = ["a", "b"];
161.           if (!validTypes.includes(data.params.subtype)) {
162.             errors.push({field: "subtype", message: "Invalid"});
163.           }
164.         }
165.       }
166.       // ... more nested conditions
167.       return {valid: errors.length === 0, errors};
168.     }
169.     
170.     // ✅ GOOD: Low complexity (extract helpers)
171.     async validateData(data: Data): Promise<Result> {
172.       const errors = [];
173.       
174.       // Early return for structure errors
175.       if (!data || typeof data !== "object") {
176.         return {valid: false, errors: [{field: "data", message: "Invalid"}]};
177.       }
178.       
179.       // Extract each validation into helper
180.       errors.push(...this.validateName(data.name));
181.       errors.push(...this.validateType(data));
182.       // ... more helper calls
183.       
184.       return {valid: errors.length === 0, errors};
185.     }
186.     
187.     private validateName(name: string): ValidationError[] {
188.       // Simple, focused function (complexity: 2)
189.       if (!name || name.length === 0) {
190.         return [{field: "name", message: "Empty"}];
191.       }
192.       if (name.length > 100) {
193.         return [{field: "name", message: "Too long"}];
194.       }
195.       return [];
196.     }
197.     ```
198. 
199.   - **Naming helper functions**: Use descriptive names that explain WHAT is being validated/processed
200. 
201. - **noUndeclaredVariables**: All variables MUST be declared before use
202.   - Ensure all imports are present at the top of the file
203.   - Check TypeScript compilation errors for missing imports
204. 
205. #### Test Quality Rules
206. 
207. - **noDuplicateTestHooks**: Don't use duplicate test hooks (beforeEach, afterEach, etc.)
208. - **noDisabledTests**: Don't commit disabled tests (`.skip`, `.todo`)
209.   - If a test must be skipped temporarily, document WHY in comments and create a tracking issue
210. 
211. ### Documentation
212. 
213. - Public APIs MUST include JSDoc comments
214. - Complex algorithms MUST include explanatory comments
215. - README files MUST be kept up-to-date with feature changes
216. - Use `// biome-ignore` comments ONLY when absolutely necessary with clear justification
217. 
218. ### Error Handling
219. 
220. - All async operations MUST handle errors explicitly
221. - User-facing errors MUST be actionable and clear
222. - Internal errors MUST include sufficient debug context
223. - Console.error() for errors that need debugging context
224. - Don't silence errors without logging
225. 
226. ### Common Coding Patterns
227. 
228. #### Constants and Configuration
229. 
230. - Define constants at the module top level, not inside functions
231. - Group related constants together
232. - Use SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE for constants
233. - Export constants that are used across modules
234. 
235. ```typescript
236. // ✅ GOOD: Top-level constants
237. const AGENT_FILE_PATTERN = /\.agent\.md$/;
238. const DEBOUNCE_DELAY_MS = 500;
239. const MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS = 3;
240. 
241. function processFile(filename: string) {
242.   return filename.replace(AGENT_FILE_PATTERN, "");
243. }
244. ```
245. 
246. #### Variable Naming
247. 
248. - Use descriptive names that avoid shadowing
249. - Prefer `item`, `element`, `entry` over reusing the collection name
250. - For callbacks, use descriptive parameter names
251. 
252. ```typescript
253. // ❌ BAD: Variable shadowing
254. const users = getUsers();
255. users.forEach((users) => { /* confusing */ });
256. 
257. // ✅ GOOD: Clear naming
258. const users = getUsers();
259. users.forEach((user) => { /* clear */ });
260.

1. # GatomIA (Agentic Spec-Driven Development Toolkit)
2. 
3. [![Visual Studio Marketplace](https://img.shields.io/vscode-marketplace/v/EITA.gatomia.svg?style=flat-square)](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=EITA.gatomia)
4. [![Downloads](https://img.shields.io/vscode-marketplace/d/EITA.gatomia.svg?style=flat-square)](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=EITA.gatomia)
5. [![GitHub stars](https://img.shields.io/github/stars/eitatech/gatomia-vscode.svg?style=flat-square)](https://github.com/eitatech/gatomia-vscode/stargazers)
6. [![GitHub issues](https://img.shields.io/github/issues/eitatech/gatomia-vscode.svg?style=flat-square)](https://github.com/eitatech/gatomia-vscode/issues)
7. 
8. GatomIA is a VS Code extension for agentic, spec-driven development with
9. first-class support for **SpecKit**, **OpenSpec**, **GitHub Copilot**, ACP
10. providers, workflow automation, and repository documentation.
11. 
12. It gives you a single workspace for authoring specs, reviewing changes,
13. previewing and refining documents, managing prompts/agents/skills, automating
14. work with hooks, and delegating implementation to local or cloud agents.
15. 
16. ---
17. 
18. ## Current Project Status
19. 
20. This project is under **continuous development**. Some features may still exhibit unexpected behavior, contain bugs, or be only partially implemented.
21. 
22. **Bug reports, suggestions, and general feedback are highly appreciated!** They play a crucial role in helping us stabilize and improve the extension.
23. 
24. We are actively working on:
25. 
26. * **Stabilizing all existing features**
27. * **Implementing the upcoming functionalities from our roadmap**
28. * **Improving overall reliability and user experience**
29. 
30. You can follow our progress, open issues, or contribute directly through our official repository:
31. 
32. **[https://github.com/eitatech/gatomia-vscode/issues](https://github.com/eitatech/gatomia-vscode/issues)**
33. 
34. ## Features
35. 
36. ### Views at a glance
37. 
38. | View | Purpose |
39. | --- | --- |
40. | **Specs** | Create specs, inspect review state, open documents in preview, and run tasks locally or on cloud agents. |
41. | **Actions** | Manage prompts, agents, skills, scripts, templates, and bundled SpecKit resources from one tree. |
42. | **Steering** | Maintain project/user instruction rules, constitutions, and global resource access policies. |
43. | **Hooks** | Create automations that react to SpecKit/OpenSpec operations and external tool events. |
44. | **Repo Wiki** | Browse and synchronize `docs/` content directly from the sidebar. |
45. | **Cloud Agents** | Track provider-backed sessions, task progress, external links, and pull requests. |
46. | **Running Agents** | Manage live ACP/Agent Chat sessions, recent runs, and orphaned worktrees. |
47. | **Quick Access** | Jump to spec system selection, settings, MCP config, dependency setup, and help. |
48. 
49. ### Spec authoring, execution, and review
50. 
51. - **Multi-system support**: Work with **SpecKit** or **OpenSpec**, or let the
52.   extension auto-detect the active system.
53. - **Spec creation**: Run `GatomIA: Create New Spec` to capture summary, product
54.   context, and constraints, then open a ready-to-send prompt in chat.
55. - **Task execution**: Run individual tasks, task groups, or an entire spec from
56.   the **Specs** tree.
57. - **Cloud delegation**: Dispatch a task group or full spec to a configured
58.   cloud provider.
59. - **Review lifecycle**: Specs move through `current -> review -> reopened ->
60.   archived`, with unarchive support for previously completed work.
61. - **Change requests**: Track reviewer feedback, archival blockers, linked tasks,
62.   and pending review work directly inside the spec explorer.
63. - **Review metadata**: The extension tracks pending tasks/checklist items so
64.   review state stays synchronized with the underlying documents.
65. 
66. ### Document preview and refinement
67. 
68. - **Rich preview panel**: Open spec, plan, task, research, checklist, and code
69.   files in a read-only preview webview.
70. - **Markdown and code support**: Markdown documents are parsed into sections and
71.   metadata; non-markdown files are rendered with syntax-aware code previews.
72. - **Frontmatter-powered forms**: Preview documents can surface structured forms
73.   and persist their submissions.
74. - **Outdated document detection**: Dependency changes can mark a document as
75.   stale so you know when downstream docs need attention.
76. - **In-place refinement requests**: Send review findings from the preview panel
77.   to the right workflow command (`/speckit.specify`, `/speckit.plan`,
78.   `/speckit.tasks`, `/speckit.research`, `/speckit.checklist`, or
79.   `/speckit.clarify`).
80. - **Editor handoff**: Jump from preview back to the source document when you
81.   want to edit the file directly.
82. 
83. ### Actions, prompts, agents, and resources
84. 
85. - **Unified Actions explorer**: Browse project prompts, agents, skills,
86.   scripts, templates, and the repository's SpecKit assets in one place.
87. - **Create built-in resources**: Create agents, skills, and Copilot prompts
88.   directly from the tree view.
89. - **Run and maintain prompts**: Execute runnable prompts from the explorer, then
90.   rename or delete them from context menus.
91. - **Copilot agent integration**: Discover agent resources from the configured
92.   resource path, hot-reload updates, and expose built-in help for agent
93.   commands.
94. - **Configurable resource loading**: Set the resource root, enable/disable
95.   hot-reload, and tune agent log verbosity.
96. 
97. ### Steering and governance
98. 
99. - **Project and user rules**: Manage `.github/instructions/*.instructions.md`
100.   and `$HOME/.github/instructions/*.instructions.md` guidance from the
101.   **Steering** view.
102. - **Constitution support**: Generate and maintain SpecKit constitutions or
103.   OpenSpec-style steering documents.
104. - **Global resource access control**: Decide whether workspace sessions can read

 GatomIA is a VS Code extension for agentic, spec-driven development with
 first-class support for **SpecKit**, **OpenSpec**, **GitHub Copilot**, ACP
 providers, workflow automation, and repository documentation.
 
 It gives you a single workspace for authoring specs, reviewing changes,
 previewing and refining documents, managing prompts/agents/skills, automating
 work with hooks, and delegating implementation to local or cloud agents.
 
 ---
 
 ## Current Project Status
 
 This project is under **continuous development**. Some features may still exhibit unexpected behavior, contain bugs, or be only partially implemented.
 
 **Bug reports, suggestions, and general feedback are highly appreciated!** They play a crucial role in helping us stabilize and improve the extension.
 
 We are actively working on:
 
 * **Stabilizing all existing features**
 * **Implementing the upcoming functionalities from our roadmap**
 * **Improving overall reliability and user experience**
 
 You can follow our progress, open issues, or contribute directly through our official repository:
 
 **[https://github.com/eitatech/gatomia-vscode/issues](https://github.com/eitatech/gatomia-vscode/issues)**
 
 ## Features
 
 ### Views at a glance
 
 | View | Purpose |
 | --- | --- |
 | **Specs** | Create specs, inspect review state, open documents in preview, and run tasks locally or on cloud agents. |
 | **Actions** | Manage prompts, agents, skills, scripts, templates, and bundled SpecKit resources from one tree. |
 | **Steering** | Maintain project/user instruction rules, constitutions, and global resource access policies. |
 | **Hooks** | Create automations that react to SpecKit/OpenSpec operations and external tool events. |
 | **Repo Wiki** | Browse and synchronize `docs/` content directly from the sidebar. |
 | **Cloud Agents** | Track provider-backed sessions, task progress, external links, and pull requests. |
 | **Running Agents** | Manage live ACP/Agent Chat sessions, recent runs, and orphaned worktrees. |
 | **Quick Access** | Jump to spec system selection, settings, MCP config, dependency setup, and help. |
 
 ### Spec authoring, execution, and review
 
 - **Multi-system support**: Work with **SpecKit** or **OpenSpec**, or let the
   extension auto-detect the active system.
 - **Spec creation**: Run `GatomIA: Create New Spec` to capture summary, product
   context, and constraints, then open a ready-to-send prompt in chat.
 - **Task execution**: Run individual tasks, task groups, or an entire spec from
   the **Specs** tree.
 - **Cloud delegation**: Dispatch a task group or full spec to a configured
   cloud provider.
 - **Review lifecycle**: Specs move through `current -> review -> reopened ->
   archived`, with unarchive support for previously completed work.
 - **Change requests**: Track reviewer feedback, archival blockers, linked tasks,
   and pending review work directly inside the spec explorer.
 - **Review metadata**: The extension tracks pending tasks/checklist items so
   review state stays synchronized with the underlying documents.

 - **Rich preview panel**: Open spec, plan, task, research, checklist, and code
   files in a read-only preview webview.

   metadata; non-markdown files are rendered with syntax-aware code previews.

   and persist their submissions.

   stale so you know when downstream docs need attention.

   to the right workflow command (`/speckit.specify`, `/speckit.plan`,
   `/speckit.tasks`, `/speckit.research`, `/speckit.checklist`, or
   `/speckit.clarify`).

   directly from the tree view.

   rename or delete them from context menus.

   resource path, hot-reload updates, and expose built-in help for agent
   commands.

   hot-reload, and tune agent log verbosity.

 - **Operational tooling**: Add, edit, enable, disable, delete, export, import,

 - **Cloud compatibility**: Devin is also available as a selectable cloud-agent

 | **Actions** | Manage prompts, agents, skills, scripts, templates, and bundled SpecKit resources from one tree. |
 | **Steering** | Maintain project/user instruction rules, constitutions, and global resource access policies. |
 | **Hooks** | Create automations that react to SpecKit/OpenSpec operations and external tool events. |
 | **Repo Wiki** | Browse and synchronize `docs/` content directly from the sidebar. |
 | **Cloud Agents** | Track provider-backed sessions, task progress, external links, and pull requests. |
 | **Running Agents** | Manage live ACP/Agent Chat sessions, recent runs, and orphaned worktrees. |
 | **Quick Access** | Jump to spec system selection, settings, MCP config, dependency setup, and help. |
 
 ### Spec authoring, execution, and review
 
 - **Multi-system support**: Work with **SpecKit** or **OpenSpec**, or let the
   extension auto-detect the active system.
 - **Spec creation**: Run `GatomIA: Create New Spec` to capture summary, product
   context, and constraints, then open a ready-to-send prompt in chat.
 - **Task execution**: Run individual tasks, task groups, or an entire spec from
   the **Specs** tree.
 - **Cloud delegation**: Dispatch a task group or full spec to a configured
   cloud provider.
 - **Review lifecycle**: Specs move through `current -> review -> reopened ->
   archived`, with unarchive support for previously completed work.
 - **Change requests**: Track reviewer feedback, archival blockers, linked tasks,
   and pending review work directly inside the spec explorer.
 - **Review metadata**: The extension tracks pending tasks/checklist items so
   review state stays synchronized with the underlying documents.
 
 ### Document preview and refinement
 
 - **Rich preview panel**: Open spec, plan, task, research, checklist, and code
   files in a read-only preview webview.
 - **Markdown and code support**: Markdown documents are parsed into sections and
   metadata; non-markdown files are rendered with syntax-aware code previews.
 - **Frontmatter-powered forms**: Preview documents can surface structured forms
   and persist their submissions.
 - **Outdated document detection**: Dependency changes can mark a document as
   stale so you know when downstream docs need attention.
 - **In-place refinement requests**: Send review findings from the preview panel
   to the right workflow command (`/speckit.specify`, `/speckit.plan`,
   `/speckit.tasks`, `/speckit.research`, `/speckit.checklist`, or
   `/speckit.clarify`).
 - **Editor handoff**: Jump from preview back to the source document when you
   want to edit the file directly.
 
 ### Actions, prompts, agents, and resources
 
 - **Unified Actions explorer**: Browse project prompts, agents, skills,
   scripts, templates, and the repository's SpecKit assets in one place.
 - **Create built-in resources**: Create agents, skills, and Copilot prompts
   directly from the tree view.
 - **Run and maintain prompts**: Execute runnable prompts from the explorer, then
   rename or delete them from context menus.
 - **Copilot agent integration**: Discover agent resources from the configured
   resource path, hot-reload updates, and expose built-in help for agent
   commands.
 - **Configurable resource loading**: Set the resource root, enable/disable
   hot-reload, and tune agent log verbosity.
 
 ### Steering and governance
 
 - **Project and user rules**: Manage `.github/instructions/*.instructions.md`
   and `$HOME/.github/instructions/*.instructions.md` guidance from the
   **Steering** view.
 - **Constitution support**: Generate and maintain SpecKit constitutions or
   OpenSpec-style steering documents.
 - **Global resource access control**: Decide whether workspace sessions can read
   global Copilot resources from your home directory.
 - **Custom instruction injection**: Append reusable instructions globally or for
   create-spec, task-start, and run-prompt flows.
 
 ### Hooks and workflow automation
 
 - **Before/after triggers**: Fire hooks before or after SpecKit/OpenSpec
   operations, with optional blocking before-execution behavior.
 - **Supported operations**: Automate around research, data model, design,
   specify, clarify, plan, tasks, tasks-to-issues, analyze, checklist,
   constitution, implementation, unit-test, and integration-test flows.
 - **Multiple action types**: Hooks can trigger agent, git, GitHub, MCP, custom,
   or ACP actions.
 - **Output-aware templates**: Use `$agentOutput`, `$clipboardContent`, and
   `$outputPath` in action arguments and message templates.
 - **ACP-aware setup**: Discover local ACP agents, known-agent integrations, and
   available models from the Hooks UI.
 - **Operational tooling**: Add, edit, enable, disable, delete, export, import,
   and inspect hook logs from the sidebar.
 
 ### Chat providers, running agents, and cloud sessions
 
 - **Provider routing**: `gatomia.chat.provider` can route extension prompts
   through GitHub Copilot Chat, Devin, Gemini, or auto-select based on host.
 - **ACP session modes**: Choose workspace-scoped, per-spec, or per-prompt ACP
   sessions depending on how much context sharing you want.
 - **Permission policy**: Control the default answer for ACP tool permission
   prompts and optionally enable verbose ACP logging.
 - **Running Agents view**: Start new agent chat sessions, reopen active/recent
   sessions, inspect lifecycle state, and clean up orphaned worktrees.
 - **Cloud Agents view**: Select a provider, monitor task and PR progress, open
   provider URLs, cancel active sessions, and remove old entries from the list.
 - **Provider discovery**: Re-probe ACP providers, switch providers, and open
   the ACP output channel from commands or context menus.
 
 ### Host-aware routing
 
 - **Supported hosts**: GatomIA detects VS Code, VS Code Insiders, Cursor,
   Windsurf, Antigravity, VSCodium, and Positron.
 - **Smart defaults**: In `auto` mode, prompt routing adapts to the current host:
   Windsurf prefers Devin CLI via ACP, Antigravity prefers Gemini CLI via ACP,
   and the other supported editors default to GitHub Copilot Chat.
 - **Remote-aware ACP behavior**: ACP-only routing is disabled in remote
   environments where the extension host cannot spawn the required local CLI
   subprocesses.
 
 ### Devin integration
 
 - **Task delegation**: Run a single task or all incomplete tasks with Devin.
 - **Progress tracking**: Open the dedicated Devin progress view/panel and cancel
   active sessions when needed.
 - **Credential management**: Configure Devin API credentials from within VS
   Code.
 - **Git-aware launch**: Task submission validates branch/repository state before
   starting a remote implementation run.
 - **Cloud compatibility**: Devin is also available as a selectable cloud-agent
   provider for broader session tracking workflows.
 
 ### Repo Wiki and documentation sync
 
 - **`docs/` explorer**: Browse markdown documents in `docs/` with folder-aware
   grouping and titles inferred from frontmatter, headings, or filenames.
 - **Document operations**: Open documents, refresh the tree, show the table of
   contents, update individual docs, or synchronize the full wiki.
 - **Status feedback**: Bulk synchronization surfaces progress, completion, and
   failure counts in the sidebar.
 
 ### Welcome screen and onboarding
 
 - **Guided setup**: A first-run welcome screen introduces the extension and can
   be reopened anytime with `GatomIA: Show Welcome Screen`.
 - **Dependency detection**: Checks GitHub Copilot Chat, Copilot CLI, SpecKit,
   OpenSpec, and related prerequisites.
 - **Install assistance**: Provides install steps, prerequisite hints, and quick
   actions for missing tools.
 - **Configuration editing**: Update core spec-system settings directly from the
   welcome experience.
 - **Learning resources**: Surface documentation, examples, tutorials, and
   curated links inside the extension, organized into **Getting Started**,
   **Advanced Features**, and **Troubleshooting** categories.
 - **Diagnostics**: Show recent setup and runtime issues in a single onboarding
   surface.
 
 ### Migration

   built-in migration command.

1. # Repository Guidelines
2. 
3. This file provides guidance to AI agents when working with code in this repository.
4. 
5. ## MANDATORY ACTIONS
6. 
7. - Follow Visual Studio Code Extension Guidelines provided by Microsoft present at [https://code.visualstudio.com/api/extension-guides/overview](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/extension-guides/overview)
8. - Follow Visual Studio Code UX guidelines provided by Microsoft present at [https://code.visualstudio.com/api/ux-guidelines/overview](https://code.visualstudio.com/api/ux-guidelines/overview)
9. - Follow GitHub Copilot SDK guidelines provided by GitHub present at [https://github.com/github/copilot-sdk](https://github.com/github/copilot-sdk)
10. - Always run `npm run check` before marking any task as complete. This ensures code quality and formatting standards are met.
11. - Never use emoji in source code files (from global user instructions).
12. 
13. ## Project Constitution (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
14. 
15. This project follows strict principles defined in [.specify/memory/constitution.md](.specify/memory/constitution.md):
16. 
17. ### I. Kebab-Case File Naming (MANDATORY)
18. 
19. - **All source files MUST use kebab-case** (e.g., `preview-store.ts`, `document-preview-panel.ts`)
20. - ❌ NO camelCase, PascalCase, or snake_case files
21. - Linter will fail on non-compliant filenames
22. - Exceptions: Config files requiring specific naming (`package.json`, `tsconfig.json`, `README.md`)
23. 
24. ### II. TypeScript-First Development
25. 
26. - All code MUST be TypeScript with `strict: true`
27. - No `any` types without explicit justification
28. - All public APIs MUST have complete type definitions
29. 
30. ### III. Test-First Development (TDD)
31. 
32. - Tests MUST be written BEFORE implementation
33. - Red-Green-Refactor cycle strictly enforced
34. - PRs without tests are rejected
35. - Test coverage MUST NOT decrease
36. - Integration tests required for new features
37. 
38. ### IV. Observability & Instrumentation
39. 
40. - Significant operations MUST include telemetry, logging, and error reporting
41. - Performance-critical paths MUST include instrumentation
42. - Errors MUST be logged with sufficient debug context
43. - No silent failures
44. 
45. ### V. Simplicity & YAGNI
46. 
47. - Implement only what is needed NOW
48. - No features or abstractions for potential future use
49. - Challenge unnecessary abstractions in code reviews
50. - Refactor when patterns emerge (Rule of Three)
51. 
52. ## Quick Start: Common Commands
53. 
54. ```bash
55. # Install all dependencies (root + ui)
56. npm run install:all
57. 
58. # Development workflow
59. npm run build          # Full build (prompts + extension + webview)
60. npm run watch          # TypeScript watch + webview dev server
61. npm run build-prompts  # Compile markdown prompts to TypeScript
62. npm run build:ext      # Bundle extension only (esbuild)
63. npm run build:webview  # Build React webview (Vite)
64. 
65. # Testing and Quality (MUST pass before commits)
66. npm test               # Run all tests (Vitest)
67. npm run test:watch     # Watch mode for tests
68. npm run test:coverage  # Generate coverage report
69. npm run lint           # Lint with Biome
70. npm run format         # Format code with Biome
71. npm run check          # Run all checks (lint + format validation) - MANDATORY
72. 
73. # Running specific tests
74. npm test -- path/to/test-file.test.ts           # Run single test file
75. npm test -- -t "test name pattern"               # Run tests matching pattern
76. 
77. # Packaging and Publishing
78. npm run package        # Create VSIX file for VS Code Marketplace
79. npm run compile        # Build then package (used for CI/CD)
80. 
81. # Webview development
82. npm --prefix ui run dev  # Run webview dev server in isolation
83. 
84. # Launch extension in debug mode
85. Press F5 in VS Code to launch the Extension Development Host
86. ```
87. 
88. ## Code Quality Standards
89. 
90. ### Formatting & Style
91. 
92. - Tab indentation (configured in formatter)
93. - Double quotes for strings
94. - Semicolons required
95. - `npm run format` MUST pass before commit
96. - `npm run lint` MUST pass before merge
97. 
98. ### Documentation
99. 
100. - Public APIs MUST include JSDoc comments
101. - Complex algorithms MUST include explanatory comments
102. - README files MUST be kept up-to-date with feature changes
103. 
104. ### Error Handling
105. 
106. - All async operations MUST handle errors explicitly
107. - User-facing errors MUST be actionable and clear
108. - Internal errors MUST include sufficient debug context
109. 
110. ## Architecture Overview
111. 
112. ### High-Level Structure
113. 
114. This is a VS Code extension that provides Agentic Spec-Driven Development capabilities, integrating with **SpecKit**, **OpenSpec**, and **GitHub Copilot**.
115. 
116. #### Extension (src/)
117. 
118. - **Main Entry**: `src/extension.ts` - Registers all commands, providers, and services
119. - **Features**: Domain-specific modules (specs, hooks, steering, documents)
120.   - `features/spec/` - Spec management and review flow
121.   - `features/hooks/` - MCP hooks and automation
122.   - `features/steering/` - Constitution/agents management
123.   - `features/documents/` - Document preview and refinement
124. - **Providers**: VS Code Tree Views and UI providers
125.   - `providers/spec-explorer-provider.ts` - Specs tree view
126.   - `providers/hooks-explorer-provider.ts` - Hooks tree view
127.   - `providers/prompts-explorer-provider.ts` - Prompts tree view
128. - **Services**: Core business logic
129.   - `services/prompt-loader.ts` - Loads and compiles markdown prompts
130.   - `services/document-preview-service.ts` - Handles document rendering
131. - **Panels**: Webview panels for rich UI
132.   - `panels/document-preview-panel.ts` - Preview/refinement UI
133. 
134. #### Webview (ui/)
135. 
136. - **React 18** SPA with TypeScript
137. - **Components**: Reusable UI components in `ui/src/components/`
138.   - `spec-explorer/` - Spec review flow components
139.   - `hooks-view/` - Hook management components
140.   - `ui/` - Shared UI components (buttons, forms, etc.)
141. - **Bridge**: VS Code extension ↔ webview communication via `ui/src/bridge/`
142. - **Stores**: State management in `ui/src/stores/`
143. - **Build**: Vite for bundling
144. 
145. ### Key Architectural Patterns
146. 
147. 1. **Dual-Build System**:
148.    - Extension: esbuild (Node.js bundle)
149.    - Webview: Vite (browser bundle)
150.    - Dependencies installed in both root and `ui/` directories
151. 
152. 2. **Message Passing**: Extension communicates with webview via VS Code postMessage API
153.    - Commands sent from webview → extension
154.    - State updates sent from extension → webview
155. 
156. 3. **MCP Integration**: Hooks system integrates with GitHub Copilot's Model Context Protocol
157.    - `MCPDiscoveryService` discovers available MCP servers/tools
158.    - `HookExecutor` executes MCP actions based on triggers
159. 
160. 4. **Review Flow**: Spec lifecycle management with status transitions
161.    - States: draft → review → reopened → archived
162.    - Change requests block archival until addressed
163.    - Telemetry tracks all state transitions
164. 
165. 5. **Test Configuration**: Vitest with dual dependency resolution
166.    - UI dependencies in `ui/node_modules/`
167.    - Testing libraries in root `node_modules/`
168.    - Aliases configured in `vitest.config.ts` to resolve both
169. 
170. ## Development Workflow
171. 
172. ### Adding a New Feature
173. 
174. 1. **Write tests first** (TDD is mandatory)
175.    - Unit tests: `tests/unit/`
176.    - Integration tests: `tests/integration/`
177.    - Webview tests: `ui/tests/` or `tests/unit/webview/`
178. 
179. 2. **Implement feature**
180.    - Follow kebab-case naming
181.    - Use strict TypeScript
182.    - Add telemetry/logging for significant operations
183. 
184. 3. **Before committing**
185. 
186.    ```bash
187.    npm test           # All tests must pass
188.    npm run check      # Linting and formatting must pass
189.    ```
190. 
191. 4. **File a PR**
192.    - Tests included and passing
193.    - Constitution compliance verified
194.    - No test coverage decrease
195. 
196. ### Running Tests for Specific Areas
197. 
198. ```bash
199. # Extension tests
200. npm test -- tests/unit/features/spec/
201. npm test -- tests/integration/
202. 
203. # Webview tests
204. npm test -- ui/tests/
205. npm test -- tests/unit/webview/
206. 
207. # Specific feature
208. npm test -- -t "Review Flow"
209. npm test -- -t "MCP"
210. ```
211. 
212. ## Key Technologies
213. 
214. - **VS Code Extension API**: 1.84.0+
215. - **TypeScript**: 5.3+ (target: ES2022, strict mode)
216. - **React**: 18.3+ (webview only)
217. - **Vitest**: 3.2+ (testing framework)
218. - **Biome**: Linting and formatting
219. - **esbuild**: Extension bundling
220. - **Vite**: Webview bundling
221. - **GitHub Copilot Chat**: AI integration
222. - **MCP (Model Context Protocol)**: Automation hooks
223. 
224. ## Integration Points
225. 
226. ### GitHub Copilot Chat
227. 
228. - Extension sends prompts to Copilot Chat via `sendPromptToChat()`
229. - Prompts compiled from markdown files in `src/prompts/`
230. - Custom instructions configurable per operation type
231. 
232. ### SpecKit / OpenSpec
233. 
234. - Extension detects spec system automatically
235. - Adapters in `utils/spec-kit-adapter.ts` provide unified interface
236. - SpecKit: `.specify/` directory structure
237. - OpenSpec: `openspec/` directory structure
238. 
239. ### MCP Servers
240. 
241. - Discovered via GitHub Copilot Chat configuration
242. - Hooks can trigger MCP tools (create issues, send notifications, etc.)
243. - Graceful degradation when MCP unavailable
244. 
245. ## Hooks System (011-custom-agent-hooks)
246. 
247. ### Overview
248. 
249. The hooks system enables automation by triggering actions when agent operations complete. Hooks can execute before or after agent operations and access captured output data.
250. 
251. ### Key Features
252. 
253. #### Timing Control
254. 
255. - **Before Execution**: Hooks run before agent starts
256.   - Optional blocking: Wait for hook completion before agent proceeds
257.   - Use case: Pre-flight validation, environment setup
258. - **After Execution**: Hooks run after agent completes (default)
259.   - Use case: Notifications, artifact processing, CI/CD triggers
260.
